reasoning_summary: For live run 20260421_122240_bf16_gemm_v1_b79a9bf at 46.021631 ms, the round-6 helper flattening was directionally safe but not yet structural: runtime improved by 0.510 ms, while the hot 128x128 PTX microkernel stayed pinned at 198 registers/thread, 16.64% active warps, 48.38% tensor activity, and roughly flat barrier pressure. That means round 7 should not spend another budget unit on the same helper family or on a stale restore. The best next move is to cut the remaining post-MMA scalar/live-state in the PTX export path, keep the wait-group/barrier seam as the second bounded exploit, and preserve one orthogonal 256x128 register-budget branch in the queue if the local PTX path still cannot move the occupancy floor.

family_audit.reason: Round 6 already proved that a bounded PTX-local live-state edit can shave runtime without breaking correctness, but the structured NCU still shows 198 registers/thread and 16.64% active warps. The next cheapest live-state target is the post-MMA export/store path.

Barrier stayed essentially flat at 5.94% while long-scoreboard regressed slightly, so the seam is still real. It drops to second because the latest measured improvement came from non-barrier cleanup, not from handoff retiming.
This remains the best orthogonal high-upside branch against the same occupancy floor, but it is still wider and riskier than the two bounded PTX-local exploits.
The current workload is now anchored on a measured 46.021631 ms surface, so another restore would mostly replay history instead of testing the next local bottleneck seam.

Round 6 just spent budget on this family. It improved runtime modestly, but it did not move the occupancy signature enough to justify an immediate second iteration before trying a different local lever.

directions.name: Trim PTX export/store-path scalar live state on the current 128x128 anchor
directions.hypothesis: The helper flattening win says small PTX-local cleanup is still worth budget, but the unchanged 198-register / 16.64%-active-warps signature says the remaining pressure is no longer in the compute helper front-end. The next best bounded move is to narrow row/column-derived pointer math and per-warp export temporaries after the MMA loop so the writer path carries less scalar live state into the hot PTX epilogue.
directions.expected_bottleneck: occupancy_latency_hiding_issue with tail_overhead_or_generic_path_issue concentrated in the PTX export/store path after the MMA loop

score_breakdown.reason: Best bounded next edit after the helper flattening result because it attacks the remaining scalar/live-state surface without abandoning the current measured anchor.

ranking_notes: Round 6 already showed that PTX-local cleanup can improve runtime on the current workload.
Registers and active warps stayed flat, so the next cleanup should move deeper into the post-MMA writer path rather than re-touch the same helper family.
This keeps the shared-memory footprint and hot-band geometry unchanged.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the edit cannot keep the current writer correctness path intact, or if registers/active warps stay flat while runtime regresses.

target_hotspots.explanation: The active PTX hot-band kernel is still register-limited at 198 registers per thread.
Achieved occupancy is still pinned near the floor, so any store-path cleanup must buy back latency-hiding headroom.

expected_local_changes: Push row/column-to-pointer conversion as late as possible into the final PTX writer helpers.
Narrow per-warp export temporaries so they do not stay live across the whole epilogue.
Keep the current grouped-row mapping, launch geometry, and shared export scratch size unchanged.

guardrail_metrics.reason: Scalar cleanup should not dilute the tensor path.
The point of the cleanup is to ease latency hiding, not make it worse.
Do not trade writer cleanup for a worse synchronization seam.

directions.name: Retime the PTX wait-group and CTA barrier seam on the current anchor
directions.hypothesis: Round 6 improved runtime without fixing the synchronization seam, which leaves the wait-group plus __syncthreads handoff as the next most credible local bottleneck. A careful retime can still reduce the 5.94% barrier stall without growing the shared-memory footprint or surrendering the current 198-register surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: synchronization_barrier_issue layered on top of an occupancy_latency_hiding_issue in the active 128x128 PTX hot-band kernel

score_breakdown.reason: Still a real seam, but the latest measured gain came from non-barrier cleanup, so it now sits just behind the writer-path trim.

ranking_notes: Barrier is still non-trivial at 5.94% and barely moved after round 6.
This remains the best second local exploit on the same current-workload anchor.
It stays below dir_01 because the latest evidence suggests there is still unspent scalar/live-state cleanup outside the barrier seam.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if reducing barrier stall requires a larger shared-memory footprint, reintroduces higher register pressure, or improves barrier without any runtime benefit.

target_hotspots.explanation: Barrier tuning must preserve the current lower-register surface.
Barrier stalls still consume a meaningful fraction of active issue slots.

expected_local_changes: Reschedule cp.async wait-group completion versus CTA-wide consumer sync inside the steady-state PTX loop.
Keep the two-stage shared-memory footprint fixed.
Preserve the current grouped-row mapping and the round-6 helper flattening changes.

guardrail_metrics.reason: Barrier retiming is not worth taking if it gives back register headroom.
A cleaner seam should not reduce active warps.
Do not simply swap barrier time for worse scoreboard stalls.

directions.name: Transplant the lower-register half-panel budget into the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot
directions.hypothesis: If the active 128x128 PTX path still cannot move off the 198-register / 16.64%-occupancy floor after two bounded local exploits, the next orthogonal branch should be the 256x128 pivot with the lower-register half-panel staging transplanted into the correctness-safe writer path. That is the best remaining structural move that still attacks the same occupancy bottleneck instead of replaying an old restore surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: occupancy_latency_hiding_issue addressed by a geometry and register-budget change rather than another PTX-local retime

score_breakdown.reason: Best orthogonal branch against the same register-limited floor, but it is still materially riskier than the two local PTX exploits.

ranking_notes: This remains the highest-upside non-PTX-local branch in the live queue.
It stays third because round 7 still has two smaller, better-instrumented PTX-local levers left to test first.
Use it if dir_01 and dir_02 both flatten out without moving registers or active warps.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the pivot transplant broadens the implementation beyond the hot-band branch, fails correctness, or cannot show a lower-register occupancy signature.

target_hotspots.explanation: Any geometry jump must buy back register budget, not just reshuffle the same pressure.
The whole point of the pivot is to move the occupancy floor upward.

expected_local_changes: Reuse the correctness-safe 256x128 writer path instead of reviving a stale historical restore surface.
Transplant only the lower-register half-panel staging and accumulator ownership choices into that pivot.
Keep the change limited to the hot-band branch and its launch selection.

guardrail_metrics.reason: This branch has a much wider correctness surface than the local PTX exploits.
The whole point of the transplant is to recover occupancy headroom.

notes: Round 7/100 diagnosis emitted after the round-6 helper-flattening probe improved runtime slightly without changing the core occupancy signature.
